message.content:
You are critically reviewing the design capture for recipe-bottle (RBK) heat ₣BZ
("rbk-14-citizen-model"). Two artifacts: a PADDOCK (shape only) and a MECHANICS MEMO (detail).

The PADDOCK (current committed text) is reproduced verbatim here:
----- PADDOCK BEGIN -----
undefined
----- PADDOCK END -----

The MECHANICS MEMO is the file Memos/memo-20260605-citizen-capability-model.md — READ IT.

Context you must hold:
- "Citizen" is a freshly-minted term for the KEYFILE-tier operator identity (a depot-issued
  service account holding one RBRA key). The future federation tier's identity is a "federate."
  The payor is the founder, outside both tiers.
- The model decouples identity lifecycle from capability lifecycle; capabilities are named
  "capability-sets" (IAM grant bundles in code); intent is stored in a "declared roster";
  an audit diffs declared-vs-IAM.
- The paddock holds SHAPE; the memo holds MECHANISM. Scoping/heat-chopping is INTENTIONALLY
  deferred — do NOT file "decide the scope" as a finding; that is a known open.
- A prior review pass already corrected ~11 issues, and the text above already reflects them.
  Do NOT re-report things already fixed in the current text. Focus on improvements NOT yet
  present. If you believe an already-present claim is wrong, say so explicitly.

Source documents you may cross-check (READ the ones relevant to your lens):
- Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md (committed two-tier credential plan; the model claims to supersede it)
- Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md (credential lifecycle split — the enabler)
- Memos/memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md (standalone governor-mantle tombstone-leak fix)
- Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSHR-HorizonRoadmap.adoc ("Operator federation" + envoy/embassy roadmap entries)
- cult-verb specs under Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/: Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDK-director_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDD-director_divest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRK-retriever_invest.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSDR-director_roster.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSGM-governor_mantle.adoc, Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSRA-CredentialFormat.adoc

Rules: Be a genuine, skeptical critic — only report REAL, substantiated issues, each with a
concrete fix. Cite evidence precisely (a quote, a spec name, a file:area, or a URL). It is fine
to find few issues. Do NOT pad. Prefer high-value findings. Your final output is structured data,
not a human message.

ADVERSARIALLY VERIFY this candidate finding. Independently check its evidence — READ the cited file/spec, or WEB-SEARCH the cited fact; do NOT trust the finding's own evidence text. Default to verdict "false" if you cannot substantiate it. If it is real but the stated fix is wrong or imprecise, mark "partial" and give a corrected refined_fix.

{"title":"Audit surplus-detection has no exception for Mason's legitimate verb-placed bindings","location":"memo §The audit ('Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope') vs §Scope ('cloud-native system identities (Mason...) ... reusing the capability-set abstraction')","type":"completeness","severity":"high","issue":"The memo says cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) 'sit outside' the civic ontology but 'reuse the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle,' and separately says the audit domain excludes 'non-roster principals.' These two statements are unreconciled, and the gap is operationally live: Mason holds five-plus standing RBK bindings placed by depot_levy (artifactregistry.writer, storage.objectViewer, viewer, logging.logWriter, plus tokenCreator on the CB agent) AND its repo writer binding is re-asserted inside director_invest's complete-policy-write. If Mason is a non-roster principal, the audit either (a) silently ignores a whole class of RBK-placed bindings — a blind spot in the very drift signal the audit exists to provide — or (b) if Mason 'reuses capability-sets', its bindings ARE set-defined and the audit must place Mason in some roster-like home, which the model has not designed. As written, an audit that compares declared-roster identities against actual RBK-relevant bindings will read every Mason binding as surplus-fragment (bindings belonging to no whole declared set) unless a permanent carve-out exists. No such carve-out is specified.","evidence":"memo:134 'Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.'; memo:29-31 'cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle'. RBSDE:192-197 Mason granted writer/objectViewer/viewer/logWriter; RBSDK:163 'serviceAccount:«MASON_EMAIL» with roles/artifactregistry.writer' re-asserted in director invest.","suggested_fix":"Decide and state Mason/Envoy's audit status explicitly: either (a) give cloud-native system identities their own declared roster of capability-sets so they fall inside the audit domain (the memo defers this ontological question — but the audit can't), or (b) define an explicit, named allow-set of system principals the surplus scan ignores, with the same 'log the bend, tie it to a removal condition' Pale discipline. Note the second-order hazard: option (b) makes 'is this an RBK-managed system SA' a trust input the surplus scan must get right, or an attacker-planted binding on a Mason-named principal escapes the tripwire.","confidence":"high"}

1	# Citizen Tier — Identity / Capability Mechanics
2	
3	Date: 2026-06-05
4	
5	Status: Design captured during the 260605 grooming conversation for heat ₣BZ
6	(`rbk-14-citizen-model`). The *shape* lives in the ₣BZ paddock; this memo holds the
7	*mechanism* the paddock points to. Nothing here is built yet. Amended 260605 after an
8	independent review pass (findings #1–#5, #9, #11, #12).
9	
10	## Scope and vocabulary
11	
12	This heat builds the **citizen tier** — the current, keyfile, no-org operator-credential
13	tier — restructured so its capability layer is identical to the future federation tier's, so
14	that federation plugs in later by swapping identity-kind and token mint and nothing else.
15	
16	- **Citizen** — a keyfile-tier operator identity: a depot-issued service account holding one
17	  RBRA key. Rank-neutral (governor, director, retriever are all citizens); the home of the
18	  identity, not the role. Possession of the key is the identity.
19	- **Federate** (future, not this heat) — a federation-tier operator identity: an external-IdP
20	  `principal://` subject the depot recognizes by treaty. A federate holds no key.
21	- **Payor** — the founding human (OAuth refresh token), outside both tiers; neither citizen nor
22	  federate (memo-20260527).
23	- **Capability-set** — a named bundle of IAM grants, defined in code (the role's binding list,
24	  lifted out of today's invest bodies). Tier-blind.
25	- **Declared roster** — depot-resident data mapping identity → {capability-sets held}.
26	  Tier-blind.
27	- **Holdings** — the depot's artifacts (arks/hoards/bullions). Property, not identities. The
28	  depot's civic ontology — citizens/federates (people) + holdings (property) — covers *operator*
29	  identities; cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the
30	  capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own
31	  ontological category is deferred).
32	
33	The convergence: the **capability layer** (capability-sets, grant/revoke, the declared roster,
34	the audit) is tier-blind and shared. The **identity** differs by tier (citizen vs federate) —
35	that difference is the one real seam. "Structural isomorphism" means the capability layer is
36	identical across tiers *modulo the opaque principal handle the identity layer supplies*
37	(`serviceAccount:…` vs `principal://…`); only identity-kind, that handle, and the token mint
38	(signed-JWT vs STS) differ.
39	
40	## Verb dissolution
41	
42	Today's role-keyed verbs decompose into a generic register parameterized by
43	(actor, capability-set, identity):
44	
45	| Today | Decomposes to | Notes |
46	|---|---|---|
47	| invest (director/retriever) | add-citizen + grant(set) | governor is actor |
48	| mantle (governor) | add-citizen + grant(governor-set) | payor is actor; mantle is not special |
49	| divest | revoke(all held) + remove-citizen | |
50	| roster | read the declared roster | |
51	| — | grant(set) / revoke(set) | the capability axis, tier-blind |
52	| — | rekey | keyfile-only identity-backing maintenance |
53	
54	Actor and capability-set are parameters, not verb variants. Federation reuses the capability
55	half (grant/revoke/roster) verbatim and swaps the identity half (add-federate, no rekey).
56	
57	## Identity verbs branch; capability verbs do not
58	
59	- **Capability verbs (grant/revoke)** never branch on tier — both are an IAM binding on a
60	  pre-existing identity.
61	- **Identity verbs** are tier-specific (this is the seam):
62	  - *citizen* (this heat): add = mint SA + key + deliver RBRA; rekey = rotate the key, bindings
63	    untouched; remove = revoke-all then delete the SA.
64	  - *federate* (future): add ≈ first grant (identity owned by the IdP, not minted here); no rekey
65	    (the IdP owns rotation); remove = de-register + revoke-all (the principal is not ours to
66	    delete).
67	  - *payor*: install / refresh a human OAuth refresh token.
68	- Token mint also branches (citizen: signed-JWT; federate: STS) and sits below everything.
69	
70	## The declared roster
71	
72	Why intent must be stored (the retired "derive from IAM" cinch was self-defeating once the role
73	prefix leaves the identity name):
74	
75	1. Without a name prefix, deriving the roster from IAM is lossy reverse-mapping — a director set
76	   is several bindings smeared across project + Mason SA + repo + bucket; partial and ambiguous
77	   matches become representable.
78	2. You cannot audit IAM against itself — "intent = whatever IAM says" makes intended-vs-actual a
79	   tautology that always passes, discarding the drift signal.
80	
81	Federation forces the same roster anyway (a federated principal carries no role marker;
82	memo-20260527: "the depot remains the home of the roster"), so the roster is paid once for both
83	tiers and makes routine roster identical and O(1) across tiers.
84	
85	- **Code vs data.** Capability-set *definitions* are code; the roster is *data* (memberships).
86	  The audit compares: for each (identity, declared-set), are the code-defined bindings present in
87	  IAM?
88	- **Physical home (open).** A GCS object in the depot bucket — mutable, high-churn, etag-guarded
89	  read-modify-write (like IAM policies themselves) — is the natural fit, unlike tripwire-protected
90	  RBRD. A GAR artifact is an alternative. Decide in-heat — but the home must place roster-write
91	  authority at least on par with grant authority (see the audit invariant below), so this is a
92	  security decision, not only storage.
93	- **Writers.** The governor writes director/retriever entries; the payor writes the governor
94	  entry — matching the authority hierarchy.
95	
96	## The audit
97	
98	Drift taxonomy (for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant
99	bindings):
100	
101	| State | Meaning | Likely cause | Heal |
102	|---|---|---|---|
103	| D = A | clean | — | — |
104	| deficit-whole | a declared set wholly absent in A | grant never ran / errant revoke | auto-converge |
105	| deficit-fragment | a declared set partly present | half-failed multi-scope grant | auto-converge |
106	| surplus-complete | a whole undeclared named set in A | out-of-band grant | report |
107	| surplus-fragment | bindings belonging to no whole set | tampering / shrunk definition | report |
108	
109	**Asymmetric healing = the Pale recognition test:**
110	
111	- **Deficit is ours** (our own grant failed to complete) → auto-converge up to D (re-run the
112	  idempotent grant), logged, never silent.
113	- **Surplus crossed the Pale** (IAM touched outside our verbs) → characterize, report, let a human
114	  adjudicate (legitimize → declare it, or reject → revoke it). Never auto-revoke (a stale roster
115	  would deprovision a working identity); never auto-backfill intent (re-enters the tautology). No
116	  verb auto-revokes; the destructive operation is always human-driven.
117	
118	**Invariant the asymmetry rests on.** Auto-converging deficits treats the declared roster as
119	authoritative intent, so writing the roster *upward* is itself a grant. The auto-heal is safe only
120	if roster-write authority is at least as protected as the grant verbs (setIamPolicy); otherwise it
121	is a softer escalation path than setIamPolicy. The roster's physical home (above) must satisfy that
122	ACL constraint — it is a security boundary, not a storage convenience.
123	
124	Consequences:
125	
126	- Because grant writes intent *first*, our tooling cannot produce a surplus *absent a definition
127	  change* — so a surplus is normally a clean "someone bypassed the verbs" signal. The one
128	  tooling-side surplus source is a capability-set definition *shrink* (next bullet), which strands a
129	  binding our own earlier grant placed — which is why surplus is reported, not auto-revoked,
130	  regardless of origin.
131	- A capability-set definition change decomposes into deficit-on-additions (auto-heal via
132	  roster-wide re-converge) + surplus-on-removals (report for human-confirmed revoke) — no special
133	  versioning machinery.
134	- Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope.
135	
136	## Orphan hazard and the identity marker (citizen tier only)
137	
138	With no name prefix and no SA labels, nothing marks an SA as RBK-managed — so a failed add-citizen
139	can strand a live key for an untracked SA (a credential leak you cannot find). Marker options:
140	
141	- **SA tags** (tag bindings): key-value, listable, condition-usable — the proper metadata
142	  mechanism. Caveat: SA tags are **Preview** (may change).
143	- **SA `description` sentinel** (`rbk:…`): stable fallback, used only for the orphan-sweep; the
144	  roster owns capability, the description earns exactly this one job.
145	
146	Federation has no key to leak and a stray principal binding is visible in the policy, so this
147	hazard is citizen-tier-specific.
148	
149	## Authority — who may grant which capability-set
150	
151	- **Resource-scope-split principle.** An authority boundary is IAM-real only where it coincides
152	  with a resource boundary. Payor/governor holds because billing is a separate resource that
153	  governor's `roles/owner` excludes. Governor/director does not — both live in project IAM and
154	  governor has owner over it — so "governor may grant only director/retriever" is, by itself,
155	  honor-system.
156	- **IAM can scope grants by role** (corrects the first paddock draft). The condition attribute
157	  `iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole` limits which roles a setIamPolicy-capable principal
158	  may grant/revoke:
159	  `api.getAttribute('iam.googleapis.com/modifiedGrantsByRole', []).hasOnly(['roles/x','roles/y'])`.
160	  Limits: works only on **project/folder/org** allow policies, **not** resource-level (AR repo,
161	  bucket, individual SA); ≤10 roles per condition; don't join `hasOnly` with `&&`/`||`; some
162	  services don't recognize the limit (request fails); the governor must NOT hold plain
163	  `roles/owner` (unconditional setIamPolicy) — give it a conditioned setIamPolicy instead.
164	  Google's own caveat — don't let a limited admin grant roles bearing setIamPolicy — is satisfied
165	  at *project* scope: the project-scoped director/retriever grants bear no setIamPolicy. The
166	  repo-scoped `roles/artifactregistry.repoAdmin` *does* carry
167	  `artifactregistry.repositories.setIamPolicy` — but that is exactly the resource-scope residual
168	  the detective audit covers, and modifiedGrantsByRole does not reach repo scope anyway.
169	- **Composed resolution.**
170	  - Project-scoped grants → IAM-limit on the governor (preventive, server-side).
171	  - Resource-scoped grants (repo/bucket/SA) → not IAM-limitable → detective audit covers the
172	    residual.
173	  - Topology → pull grant authority to the payor where a standing governor buys nothing.
174	- **Tier-dependent.** Keyfile/solo (payor == admin) → topology: a standing owner-holding citizen
175	  buys nothing and is pure attack surface; drop or demote it. Federation/multi-admin (no
176	  exfiltrable key) → a condition-limited governor is the right preventive tool.
177	- **Audit ↔ topology seam.** Surplus-detection's security value depends on topology: a broad
178	  standing governor makes surplus-detection the only escalation tripwire (detective-only, weak vs
179	  malice); authority at the payor closes escalation structurally and demotes the audit to
180	  honest-mistake hygiene. Do not substitute detective audit for structural closure.
181	- **Ephemeral governor** (decoupling-enabled option): grant the governor-set for one op, then
182	  revoke. Shrinks resting owner-exposure; relevant only if a keyfile delegated governor is ever
183	  wanted.
184	
185	## Blast radius — full reasoning
186	
187	One citizen per person = one SA = one key carrying the **union** of granted capabilities. This is
188	**forced** by "one identity per person": an SA's key always wields the SA's full bindings; you
189	cannot scope one key to a subset. The only escape — separate SAs per role — is the old model,
190	which discards the citizen goal. So accepting multi-role on one citizen *is* accepting the union
191	key; they are one decision.
192	
193	- Cost: larger resting target (a leaked key carries more).
194	- Bounded: the *resting-key* form of this exposure is citizen-tier-only (a file to steal);
195	  federation has no key at rest. The union-of-capabilities-on-one-identity property is itself
196	  tier-blind — a federate granted director+retriever wields both too, exposed through a hijacked
197	  live session rather than a file at rest. Most citizens wear one hat (one-per-person ==
198	  one-per-role).
199	- Upside: finer incident response — revoke a capability without touching the citizen/key, or rekey
200	  without touching capabilities. The old model's only lever was delete-and-recreate.
201	
202	## Governor teardown leak (folds in)
203	
204	`rbgp_governor_mantle` deletes outgoing `governor-*` SAs without first revoking their grants,
205	minting a `deleted:…?uid=` tombstone per re-mantle. The leak closes cheaply: add the generic
206	revoke-before-delete (reusing the rbk-08 revoke layer + Class-C propagation tolerance) before the
207	existing delete — no tombstone, no naming change. The further idempotent-governor refinement (rekey
208	instead of delete+recreate, dropping the delete entirely) is *not* free: it needs a *stable*
209	governor name in place of today's datestamped `governor-YYYYMMDDHHmm` (which exists precisely to
210	dodge the delete→recreate flap), plus a one-time migration of existing datestamped governors.
211	Standalone leak fix (if the heat descopes the governor):
212	memo-20260605-governor-mantle-tombstone-leak.md.
213	
214	## Verified facts (checked 260605)
215	
216	- **Service accounts do not support resource labels.** They support **tags** (tag bindings),
217	  currently in Preview. So the orphan marker uses tags (Preview) or the description sentinel, not a
218	  label.
219	- **IAM can limit which roles a principal may grant** via `modifiedGrantsByRole`, but only on
220	  project/folder/org policies and ≤10 roles — not on resource-level policies.
221	
222	Sources:
223	- Set limits on granting roles — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/setting-limits-on-granting-roles
224	- Tags for service accounts — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts-tags
225	- Service accounts overview — https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-account-overview
226	
227	## memo-20260527 divergence (record when the model is frozen)
228	
229	memo-20260527 states the operator verbs "keep their names, only their bodies differ," and that
230	keyfile holds "one SA key file each per role." This heat supersedes both: the verb *bodies*
231	converge in shape (not just names), and the citizen tier is one SA *per person*, not per role.
232	
233	A third divergence the paddock leans on by name: 20260527's "single code seam" is a *role-keyed*
234	token accessor ("a token as director"), shippable with no behavior change over today's per-role
235	SAs. Under one-SA-per-person the accessor becomes *identity-keyed* ("a token as alice", carrying the
236	union) — a generalization of that seam, but *not* itself no-behavior-change, since it presupposes
237	the per-person migration. (Whether the heat's "load-bearing core" is the role-keyed no-behavior
238	stepping-stone or the identity-keyed end state is an open scoping question — see the paddock.)
239	
240	Update memo-20260527 to record these when the model is frozen.
241

The candidate finding cites memo lines 134 and 29-31, and RBSDE lines 192-197 and RBSDK line 163. Let me verify each.

**Memo line 134** (verified): "Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope."

**Memo lines 29-31** (verified): "cloud-native system identities (Mason, future Envoy) sit outside it, reusing the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle (whether they deserve their own ontological category is deferred)."

**RBSDE lines 192-197** (verified): Mason granted `roles/artifactregistry.writer` (repo), `roles/storage.objectViewer` (bucket), `roles/viewer` and `roles/logging.logWriter` (project). Plus line 202-205: tokenCreator on Mason from CB agent.

**RBSDK lines 161-165** (verified): Complete-policy-write re-asserts `serviceAccount:«MASON_EMAIL»` with `roles/artifactregistry.writer` to prevent stale read from overwriting Mason's binding set by depot_levy.

Now let me carefully examine the logical claim. The finding asserts two memo statements are "unreconciled." Let me re-read them in context.

The memo line 134 says the audit domain is "roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope." This is actually an explicit scoping statement — Mason is not in the declared roster (it's a cloud-native system identity, line 29-31 explicitly places it outside the civic ontology and outside the citizen lifecycle), so Mason's bindings are out of scope. That's coherent on its face.

Let me check whether the memo's "reusing the capability-set abstraction" actually implies Mason's bindings must be audited. Lines 29-31 say system identities reuse the capability-set *abstraction* "without the citizen lifecycle." The roster maps identity → capability-sets held (line 25). The audit (line 88) compares "for each (identity, declared-set)." Mason has no declared-roster entry, so under line 134 it's simply excluded.

Let me check the finding's claim about "surplus-fragment" reading Mason bindings.

1	# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-27
4	
5	Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
6	per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
7	organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
8	the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
9	single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
10	roadmap entry.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## The plan
15	
16	RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:
17	
18	- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
19	  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
20	- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
21	  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.
22	
23	A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
24	exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
25	codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
26	requirement, which is also the natural paywall.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## Two models, two tiers
31	
32	| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
33	|---|---|---|
34	| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
35	| GCP organization | not required | required |
36	| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
37	| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
38	| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |
39	
40	Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
41	Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
42	model-blind.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## The organization requirement is the tier boundary
47	
48	Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
49	**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
50	structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.
51	
52	An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
53	creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
54	tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
55	pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
56	verify a domain.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products
61	
62	GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
63	authenticating?*
64	
65	| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
66	|---|---|---|
67	| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
68	| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
69	| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |
70	
71	RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
72	actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## How federation authenticates
77	
78	The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
79	touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
80	**never runtime**.
81	
82	**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
83	workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
84	issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
85	claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
86	receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
87	`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
88	Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
89	already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
90	roster.
91	
92	**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
93	IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
94	(RFC 8628):
95	
96	1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
97	   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
98	2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
99	   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
100	   is not involved.
101	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.
102	
103	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**
104	
105	```
106	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
107	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
108	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
109	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
110	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
111	  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
112	```
113	
114	Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
115	no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.
116	
117	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
118	1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
119	and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
120	long session.
121	
122	---
123	
124	## IdP landscape
125	
126	Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
127	Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
128	practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
129	The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
130	Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
131	IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.
132	
133	---
134	
135	## Refresh-token policy
136	
137	A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
138	Confine it to exactly one place:
139	
140	- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
141	  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
142	- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
143	  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
144	  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
145	  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
146	  token.
147	- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
148	  outside the operator mode enum.
149	
150	A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
151	on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.
152	
153	---
154	
155	## Where configuration lives
156	
157	Three homes, by scope:
158	
159	- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
160	  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
161	  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
162	  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
163	  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
164	  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
165	  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
166	  independently straddle modes.
167	- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
168	  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
169	  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
170	- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
171	  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.
172	
173	There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
174	the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
175	to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
176	secret.
177	
178	---
179	
180	## Operator-admin verbs
181	
182	The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
183	**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
184	operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
185	keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
186	on the operator's `principal://` subject.
187	
188	Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
189	**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
190	trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
191	enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.
192	
193	---
194	
195	## One consumer path, real and synthetic
196	
197	Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
198	resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
199	only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
200	device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
201	token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
202	path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.
203	
204	---
205	
206	## The single code seam
207	
208	Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
209	token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —
210	
211	- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
212	- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.
213	
214	Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
215	adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.
216	
217	Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
218	pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
219	enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
220	call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
221	second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
222	again.
223	
224	**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
225	the federation branch.**
226	
227	---
228	
229	## Open at implementation time
230	
231	- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
232	- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
233	  → what the token resolver consumes).
234	- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
235	  calls.
236	
237	---
238	
239	## Sources
240	
241	- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
242	- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
243	- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
244	- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
245	- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
246	- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)
247

1	[[rbshr_horizon_roadmap]]
2	==== Horizon Roadmap
3	
4	Defined-but-unscoped future work across Recipe Bottle.
5	Each item has a rationale (why it matters), a trigger (when to revisit), and a reference (where the evidence lives).
6	
7	Items here are not planned for any heat.
8	When an item becomes actionable, it moves into a heat as paces with proper dockets.
9	This is the single collection point for big changes — individual spec subdocuments should cross-reference here rather than maintaining local "Future Work" sections.
10	
11	**SA numeric ID for member references.**
12	Google recommends using a
13	{giam_service_account}'s
14	numeric ID rather than its email address when referencing it immediately after creation, to reduce propagation delay.
15	Recipe Bottle uses email addresses throughout, which is why propagation retry
16	(<<rbscip_trade_study>>
17	backoff profile) is necessary.
18	Switching to numeric ID could reduce retry frequency but would require threading the numeric ID from SA creation responses through all call chains that construct `serviceAccount:` member strings.
19	Requires verification that `setIamPolicy` accepts numeric ID in member strings.
20	_Trigger_: if propagation retries become a significant wall-clock cost in depot creation or governor reset.
21	_Reference_:
22	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
23	(SA propagation section, evidence table).
24	
25	**Read-back verification universalization.**
26	Currently only
27	{rbtoe_iam_grant_project}
28	performs post-write verification (re-reading policy to confirm the granted role is observable).
29	{rbtoe_iam_grant_repo},
30	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa},
31	and
32	{rbtoe_iam_grant_bucket}
33	trust the 200 from `setIamPolicy`.
34	Adding verification universally would provide stronger guarantees but adds an HTTP round-trip and a new timeout window per grant.
35	_Trigger_: after etag convergence (\u20a2AsAAF) has been validated in production and we have confidence in the corrected grant contracts.
36	_Reference_:
37	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
38	(Convergence Analysis section).
39	
40	**SA preflight universalization.**
41	Currently only
42	{rbtoe_iam_grant_sa}
43	performs a pre-flight existence check on the target
44	{giam_service_account}.
45	Applying it to all grant types when the member is a service account requires conditional logic and changes behavior for non-SA members.
46	_Trigger_: if grant failures due to non-existent members become a diagnostic problem.
47	_Reference_:
48	<<rbscig_iam_grant_contracts>>
49	(Convergence Analysis section).
50	
51	**Podman as first-class deployment target.**
52	Recipe Bottle currently deploys to Podman on workstations via the bottle lifecycle (RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL).
53	Podman support is functional but not hardened — the sentry container, network isolation, and eBPF enforcement assume Linux Podman with rootful access.
54	Expanding to rootless Podman, Podman on macOS (via podman-machine), or alternative container runtimes would require revisiting the security model and network architecture.
55	_Trigger_: user demand for rootless deployment or macOS-native workflows beyond the current Podman VM approach.
56	_Reference_: RBSPV-PodmanVmSupplyChain.adoc (current VM supply chain);
57	RBSBS, RBSBR, RBSBL (bottle lifecycle specs).
58	
59	**`podman-compose` lacks `--wait` for `compose up`.**
60	Docker Compose `--wait` (v2.1.1+) blocks until all container health checks pass, which `rbob_charge` relies on to guarantee crucible readiness before returning.
61	`podman-compose` does not implement `--wait` — the command returns immediately after scheduling containers, creating a race between charge completion and the active-check assertion in theurge.
62	Without `--wait`, Podman integration will need an alternative readiness gate (polling loop, explicit health-check query, or `podman wait --condition=healthy`).
63	_Trigger_: Podman first-class deployment (above).
64	_Reference_: containers/podman-compose#710 (June 2023, open);
65	containers/podman-compose#1329 (October 2025, open).
66	
67	**Revised enshrine — universal capture verb under Lode recipe noun.**
68	Today's narrow `enshrine` mirrors only upstream OCI base images.
69	Three additional capture needs share the same gesture (pull from upstream, SLSA-wrap, store in GAR): build-time tool images (today's `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery), the WSL substrate distro that hosts the Windows workload, and podman VM disk images.
70	The revision generalizes `enshrine` into a universal verb operating on a single recipe noun — **Lode**, parallel to {rbtga_vessel} on the built side — with a kind enum that gates pipeline behavior: `base`, `tool`, `reliquary`, `wsl`, `podvm-wsl`, `podvm-native`.
71	Today's `reliquary` demotes from top-level noun to a kind enum slot — the date-cohort semantic survives, the cult-vocabulary weight drops.
72	Today's `mirror` verb retires entirely;
73	absorbed into enshrine on the `tool` and `reliquary` kinds.
74	Lode .env carries author intent only — no resolved FQIN field — mirroring RBRV's ORIGIN/ANCHOR pattern (kindle code computes upstream coordinates from kind primitives at module-startup).
75	The `wsl` kind delivers a project-controlled WSL rootfs tar that replaces the Microsoft-Store-appx-mutable distribution today's caparison-w depends on;
76	the `podvm-*` kinds collapse `rbv_PodmanVM.sh`'s existing GHCR mirror into GAR under the unified verb.
77	_Trigger_: when Windows host workload requires a project-controlled WSL distro, when podman VM supply chain needs project-owned bytes in GAR, or when the `mirror`/`reliquary` machinery accumulates enough friction to motivate consolidation.
78	_Reference_: RBSAE-ark_enshrine.adoc (current narrow-scope spec);
79	RBSRV-RegimeVessel.adoc (ORIGIN/ANCHOR precedent, kind-enum-gated variable groups pattern);
80	`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/FUTURE/rbv_PodmanVM.sh` (existing podman-vm fan-out machinery).
81	
82	**Hairpin peer access between crucibles.**
83	Bottled containers cannot currently reach each other's published entry ports.
84	Each bottle is isolated behind its own sentry on a separate enclave network.
85	The traffic path is feasible: Bottle A egresses through Sentry A's transit bridge, the host kernel routes to Sentry B's transit bridge, and Sentry B's existing entry path (DNAT + RBM-FORWARD) accepts the connection unchanged.
86	The security model is preserved — the sentry remains the sole enforcement point, with peer access as a new category of allowed destination alongside entry (inbound) and uplink (outbound internet).
87	Nameplate model: two new variables (`RBRN_PEER_ACCESS_MODE` disabled|allowlist, gated `RBRN_PEER_ALLOWED_MONIKERS` as comma-separated moniker list).
88	No global mode — peer access must always be explicit.
89	Implementation touches three areas: nameplate kindle/validate/render, a peer-access iptables step in security_config, and host-side peer IP discovery via container inspection.
90	Audit gains a cross-instance check: each named peer must exist and have entry enabled.
91	_Trigger_: when a use case requires bottle-to-bottle communication (e.g., microservice composition, service mesh patterns).
92	_Reference_: prior design conversation capture (paddock).
93	
94	**Hoard: external artifact mirror in retention-locked GCS.**
95	A
96	{rbtgi_depot}
97	citizen alongside the
98	{rbtga_ark}
99	that mirrors external content-addressed corpora — canonical case: Hugging Face model checkpoints.
100	GCS archive is ~80× cheaper per byte than GAR and supports bucket-level retention locks for compliance-grade immutability that GAR does not offer.
101	Ingress reuses upstream SHA256 addressing (shallow git + LFS pointer resolution, SHA256-keyed GCS objects with natural dedup across revisions);
102	Cloud Build streams so egress stays Google-side;
103	auth path reuses the existing JWT/OAuth bearer curl stack — no new tooling dependency.
104	+
105	Intended consumption: just-in-time workstation mount from the retention-locked bucket into the
106	{at_bottle_container}
107	at charge time, rather than baking models into
108	{at_bottle_image_s}.
109	Keeps images lean, enables model swap without rebuild, keeps the retention property where it naturally lives.
110	Tradeoff: provenance becomes a
111	{at_nameplate}-declared
112	hoard digest recorded at charge time rather than a SLSA-attested image layer — weaker by one link.
113	_Trigger_: first concrete need to run LM inference inside a
114	{at_bottle_container}
115	against a specific HF checkpoint with retention guarantees.
116	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
117	design conversation pending heat.
118	
119	**CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains.**
120	The sentry's iptables CIDR rules accept all protocols and all destination ports to allowed IP ranges.
121	This is intentional: the spec defines
122	{rbrn_uplink_access_mode_allowlist}
123	as "IP access restricted to allowed CIDR ranges" — not port-restricted.
124	The DNS allowlist
125	({rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains})
126	provides name-level precision;
127	the CIDR allowlist provides IP-level gating.
128	These two layers are independent and reinforce each other.
129	+
130	The weakness emerges when allowed domains are CDN-hosted.
131	IANA's example.com (RFC 2606) migrated to Cloudflare, requiring CIDR expansion from `93.184.216.0/24` to include `104.16.0.0/12` — a /12 covering ~1M IPs shared with a large fraction of Cloudflare-fronted sites.
132	The DNS allowlist remains precise (two domains), but the CIDR backstop is now porous: any destination within the Cloudflare range is reachable regardless of domain.
133	A compromised or attacker-controlled site sharing the CDN range would be reachable from the bottle.
134	+
135	Three potential mitigations, in order of increasing ambition:
136	+
137	. **Accept as-is with documentation.**
138	  The DNS allowlist is the primary gate;
139	  the CIDR is a coarse backstop.
140	  Document the known weakness for broad CIDRs.
141	  Suitable when the allowed domains are trusted and the CDN range is acceptable collateral exposure.
142	. **Dynamic CIDR from DNS resolution.**
143	  Have dnsmasq feed resolved IPs into iptables at resolution time, so the allowed set tracks actual DNS responses rather than static configuration.
144	  Eliminates CDN range sprawl.
145	  Requires new sentry infrastructure (dnsmasq hook scripts or ipset integration).
146	. **TLS SNI enforcement.**
147	  Inspect TLS ClientHello for the SNI field and enforce that it matches the DNS allowlist.
148	  Closes the CDN gap at the application layer without dynamic iptables.
149	  Requires a transparent TLS proxy or eBPF-based SNI extraction in the sentry — a different order of complexity.
150	+
151	_Trigger_: when allowed domains require CIDRs broader than /24, or when the threat model requires preventing lateral reachability within a CDN range.
152	_Reference_: rbjs_sentry.sh (Phase 3 CIDR rules);
153	the tadmor nameplate's {rbrn_regime} assignment file (current CIDR configuration).
154	
155	**Encrypted tunnel conduit (WireGuard in sentry).**
156	Add WireGuard to the sentry image so the sentry terminates an encrypted tunnel directly to a VPC hosting PrivateLink endpoints for cloud services.
157	The bottle's traffic routes through a `wg0` interface on the sentry — one stable VPC CIDR replaces floating cloud-provider IP ranges.
158	WireGuard is ~400KB on Alpine, uses static keypair exchange (no PKI/CA), and fits naturally into the sentry's existing privileged-container, explicit-routing architecture.
159	New nameplate fields: tunnel mode, peer endpoint, public keys, allowed IPs.
160	VPC side requires a small WireGuard peer instance and PrivateLink endpoints for target services.
161	_Trigger_: when a bottle needs to reach a cloud service that supports PrivateLink (AWS Bedrock is the known first case; survey should include Vertex AI, Azure OpenAI, GitLab Dedicated, SageMaker, and others as they emerge).
162	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (full design exploration, approach comparison, service compatibility survey).
163	
164	**Cloud service conduit nameplate.**
165	A nameplate configuration pattern for bottles that access cloud services at runtime.
166	Near-term: allowlist-only nameplate targeting specific service CIDRs and domains (works today with existing sentry machinery, no development needed).
167	Longer-term: tunnel-mode nameplate using WireGuard conduit (above).
168	AWS Bedrock is the known requirement;
169	the pattern should be researched broadly across cloud AI inference services, managed Git hosting, and other PrivateLink-capable endpoints.
170	_Trigger_: immediate — Bedrock access is a current need.
171	Allowlist-only nameplate can be created now;
172	tunnel-mode nameplate follows WireGuard sentry work.
173	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (approach A for allowlist-only, approach C for tunnel mode).
174	
175	**Bottle credential custody.**
176	Move the service secrets a bottle workload uses — cloud API keys, IAM keys, SSH keys — off the operator's workstation and into the bottle itself, injected at charge time via nameplate/regime configuration rather than baked into the bottle image (a published image must never carry a live secret).
177	The workstation starts the crucible but never holds the service secret; its only credential becomes permission to charge.
178	This blocks credential theft from a compromised workstation — the one threat the sentry allowlist and the encrypted tunnel do not already close — by shrinking the secret's resting place to the contained bottle.
179	The existing RBRA file regime-path pattern provides the model.
180	Orthogonal to but naturally paired with the encrypted tunnel conduit: the tunnel grants the bottle network reach to a cloud service and the sentry custodies the tunnel key, while this item custodies the service secret inside the bottle — network access and the credential to use it configured together.
181	_Trigger_: alongside the conduit nameplate work.
182	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md` (Credential Confinement section; the `Credential theft from workstation` row of the threat-model table).
183	
184	NOTE: For SaaS services without PrivateLink (GitHub.com, GitLab.com), the sentry CIDR/domain allowlist + TLS remains the realistic ceiling.
185	The sentry still provides the core value: the bottle cannot reach anything the sentry doesn't allow, and the workstation is cut out of the data path.
186	The tunnel adds defense-in-depth only for PrivateLink-capable services.
187	Cross-reference: the existing "CIDR allowlist granularity vs CDN-hosted domains" item (Network Security Model) remains relevant for SaaS-only services where the tunnel cannot help.
188	
189	**Cross-cloud architectural stance: asymmetric sovereignty.**
190	Recipe Bottle governs identities and infrastructure only in GCP.
191	AWS-side presence is shaped as delivered artifacts and policy templates, not owned principals — no AWS-Director, no AWS-Mason, no AWS-Retriever.
192	If Recipe Bottle ever needs to *build* in AWS rather than deliver to it, that would be a deliberate architectural decision triggering a full parallel role hierarchy, not an incremental drift.
193	Vocabulary used in related entries — embassy, envoy, bullion, entrust, accredit, recall, chantry — is tentative working language from a design conversation; concepts are firmer than the words.
194	_Trigger_: when AWS-side work moves into a heat with real scope.
195	_Reference_: this roadmap entry; design conversation pending heat.
196	
197	**Embassy — AWS-side delivery premises.**
198	An S3 bucket (with Object Lock in Compliance mode for retention), an IAM role with a trust policy accepting the envoy's federated identity, and Recipe-Bottle-shipped IAM policy shapes that the AWS consumer attaches to whatever principal consumes delivered artifacts (EC2 role, ECS task role, Bedrock execution role, etc.).
199	Provisioned by the payor via operations congruent with
200	{rbtgi_depot}
201	creation — the AWS consumer's embassy is the sovereign-setup equivalent of the Manor's depot.
202	Recipe Bottle ships provisioning templates;
203	the AWS consumer owns the resulting resources.
204	_Trigger_: first concrete need to deliver artifacts to an AWS workload.
205	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
206	design conversation pending heat.
207	
208	**Envoy — GCP-side cloud-native cross-post actor.**
209	A
210	{giam_service_account}
211	in the depot, sibling to existing cloud-native SAs, whose sole purpose is executing cross-cloud pushes to an accredited embassy.
212	The envoy holds no long-lived AWS credentials;
213	it federates to AWS via OIDC Workload Identity Federation, exchanging short-lived GCP tokens for short-lived AWS STS credentials at operation time.
214	Preserves credential locality: Director holds only GCP credentials on the workstation, envoy holds only federation configuration in GCP, and AWS admin credentials never enter Recipe Bottle's custody.
215	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
216	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
217	
218	**Bullion — AWS-side content-addressed artifact.**
219	Recipe Bottle's delivered artifact in an embassy bucket: a content-addressed filesystem image (squashfs/erofs, optionally with dm-verity) with Object Lock applied for compliance-grade retention.
220	A bullion may carry a vessel image payload (the "Director pushes a vessel to AWS" motivator), model weights, or any other content-addressed corpus.
221	Sibling-but-distinct from the hoard: hoards stay GCS-native and depot-resident, preserving the existing provenance story;
222	bullions are the new AWS-side citizen with S3-native retention semantics.
223	Provenance transports as naming-convention sibling objects (attestation JSON adjacent to the image blob), not as OCI referring artifacts, because the bullion is not an OCI artifact.
224	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
225	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
226	Artifact Preservation section (hoard entry) for the GCP sibling concept.
227	
228	**Accredit / Recall — governor operations for embassy binding lifecycle.**
229	Accredit: governor configures the envoy's OIDC federation against an embassy's IAM role trust policy, and records the binding (embassy name, AWS account, region, role ARN, bucket name) in depot state.
230	Recall: governor removes the federation configuration and prunes the binding.
231	Follows the existing governor verb register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) with diplomatic-register verbs appropriate for foreign-court relationships.
232	A single depot may be accredited to multiple embassies.
233	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
234	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
235	
236	**Entrust — director-triggered cross-post operation.**
237	Director invokes entrust on a specific hallmark (or other deliverable) with a target embassy name.
238	The envoy executes the cross-post from within Cloud Build: federates to the embassy's IAM role, streams bytes to S3, applies Object Lock retention.
239	Director holds no AWS credentials;
240	failure modes surface through the existing Cloud Build event stream.
241	_Trigger_: embassy entry above.
242	_Reference_: this roadmap entry.
243	
244	**Chantry — Bedrock-hosted inference endpoint.**
245	Architecturally distinct from the embassy/bullion pattern.
246	A chantry is a Bedrock-registered custom model (or a native Bedrock foundation model) invoked by a
247	{at_bottle_container}
248	over the crucible conduit architecture — not an artifact Recipe Bottle delivers, but a foreign-sovereign service a bottle consumes.
249	Weights may originate as a bullion (entrusted to an embassy, then consumed by Bedrock Custom Model Import on the AWS side), but the chantry itself is the registered-and-invocable endpoint, not the weight storage.
250	Pairs with the "Cloud service conduit nameplate" entry for the invocation path.
251	_Trigger_: first concrete Bedrock inference requirement;
252	near-term per the Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
253	_Reference_: this roadmap entry;
254	`Memos/memo-20260415-crucible-conduit-architecture.md`;
255	Crucible Conduit Architecture section above.
256	
257	**Egress lockdown — IMPLEMENTED.**
258	Dual-pool architecture: tether pool (public egress) and airgap pool (`NO_PUBLIC_EGRESS`).
259	Operation-level routing: inscribe/enshrine use tether, about/vouch always airgap, conjure/bind per vessel `RBRV_EGRESS_MODE`.
260	Reliquaries (tool images) and anchors (base images) provide universal GAR-sourced dependencies.
261	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Implemented: Egress Lockdown via Dual Pools).
262	
263	**VPC Service Controls perimeter.**
264	Wrap depot project in VPC-SC perimeter restricting API access.
265	Requires egress lockdown (now implemented) as prerequisite, org-level admin for perimeter creation, and verification of every GCP API call in the pipeline.
266	_Trigger_: organizational security policy mandating VPC-SC, or data exfiltration risk assessment.
267	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Potential Hardening: VPC Service Controls).
268	Cross-reference: if a VPC is stood up for the crucible conduit architecture (above), design the VPC-SC perimeter to serve both Cloud Build egress and bottle conduit consumers.
269	
270	**Cosign image signing.**
271	Cryptographic signatures on images, verifiable by downstream consumers.
272	KMS-backed keyed signing preferred for airgap-pool compatibility.
273	Currently redundant given CB-native SLSA v1.0 Build Level 3 attestation.
274	_Trigger_: external image distribution, trust verification needed outside GCP, or builder strategy expanding beyond Google Cloud Build.
275	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Cosign).
276	
277	**Binary Authorization.**
278	Deploy-time policy enforcement (only attested images can be deployed).
279	Technically achievable now with CB-native SLSA Level 3.
280	Not beneficial until deployment targets include GKE or Cloud Run.
281	_Trigger_: deployment targets change from Podman workstations to managed container platforms.
282	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Binary Authorization).
283	
284	**Dockerfile base image digest validation.**
285	Builder step images are digest-pinned, but user-authored Dockerfiles in vessel build contexts are not validated.
286	A vessel with `FROM ubuntu:22.04` uses a mutable tag.
287	Constrains vessel authors (policy decision).
288	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem matures beyond internal-only use.
289	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Dockerfile base image).
290	
291	**Reproducibility verification.**
292	Periodic independent rebuilds compared by digest.
293	Requires reproducible toolchains — a vessel-author discipline, not a pipeline control.
294	_Trigger_: vessel ecosystem maturity and external distribution.
295	_Reference_: RBSCB-CloudBuildPosture.adoc (Deferred Items: Reproducibility).
296	
297	**Operator federation — external-IdP identity for depot participants.**
298	Recipe Bottle grants
299	{rbtgi_depot}
300	capabilities to identities the depot's IAM recognizes.
301	The shipping model is the keyfile tier: each operator role holds a service-account key file (the
302	{rbtgi_rbra_file})
303	on disk, possession of the key is the identity, and no GCP organization is required.
304	Operator federation is the heavyweight successor tier for installations that cannot accept an operator secret on disk.
305	The depot owner configures the
306	{rbtgi_depot}
307	to trust an external OIDC/SAML identity provider (any compliant issuer — self-hosted Dex/Keycloak, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP), then grants capabilities to those federated `principal://` subjects: keyless, no Google accounts for participants, revocation central at the depot.
308	The
309	{rbtgi_depot}
310	remains the home of the participant roster (the role-to-identity mapping); the IdP owns only identity proofing.
311	Org-only — federation pools are organization-level (Workforce Identity Federation, the human-identity product), so a no-org
312	{rbtr_payor}
313	cannot use this tier; the organization requirement is the line between the free keyfile tier and the federation tier.
314	A qualifying organization is free via Cloud Identity once its owner verifies control of a DNS domain, so the true prerequisite is that the operator controls a domain — not that they pay Google.
315	The Google-side credential path is curl-native — an STS token exchange (RFC 8693), no gcloud and no added stack dependency; only the IdP-auth leg (device flow where the issuer supports it) varies by issuer.
316	Distinct from the envoy's egress federation (GCP-to-AWS delivery, the machine-identity Workload product): this is
317	{at_operator}
318	*access* identity arriving the other way.
319	_Trigger_: first concrete need from an organization that cannot accept on-disk operator keyfiles, or when the keyfile tier's secret-distribution and rotation burden materially blocks adoption rather than being theoretically frictionful.
320	_Reference_: `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md` (the source document for this entry — the committed two-tier plan: keyfile (free, no-org) and Workforce federation (paid, org-required), the curl-native two-leg flow, device-flow / 12-hour session cap, the per-depot mode enum homed in RBRD, and the cult-verb shape);
321	`Memos/memo-20260522-org-affiliated-credential-reorientation.md` (R1/R2/R3 credential model and three-tier ladder, plus R4/R5 test-rig and gauntlet resolutions);
322	`Memos/memo-20260427-google-native-human-auth.md` (impersonation mechanism detail — its human-auth *framing* is superseded by R2);
323	Google docs: Workforce Identity Federation overview (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation`) and short-lived token exchange (`https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials`).

content.text:
Now I have the full evidence. Let me analyze the candidate finding's core logical claim carefully.

The finding claims two memo statements are "unreconciled":
1. Line 134: "Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope."
2. Lines 29-31: system identities (Mason) "reusing the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle."

The finding argues these are contradictory: if Mason is non-roster (out of audit scope), then either (a) the audit has a blind spot for RBK-placed bindings, or (b) Mason "reuses capability-sets" so its bindings ARE set-defined and must be in some roster.

Let me examine whether this is actually a contradiction or a coherent design.

The memo's audit model (lines 98-134) is keyed by **identity in the declared roster**. The drift taxonomy (line 98) is "for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant bindings." The audit iterates over roster identities and, *for each*, diffs declared-vs-actual. Line 134 says the domain is "roster-identities × set-defined bindings" — meaning the audit visits each roster identity and checks the bindings that identity's declared sets define.

This is **per-identity-keyed**, not a global scan of all bindings in the project. The finding's central claim — that "an audit that compares declared-roster identities against actual RBK-relevant bindings will read every Mason binding as surplus-fragment" — assumes the surplus scan looks at the *whole project policy* and flags any binding not attributable to a declared set. But that is **not** what line 134 describes. Line 134 explicitly restricts the audit's reach to roster identities. Mason, being non-roster, is never visited; its bindings are never examined; they cannot be classified as surplus-fragment because surplus is defined *per roster identity* (line 106-108: "for one identity").

So the finding's premise — that the surplus scan iterates over all RBK-relevant bindings project-wide — contradicts the actual design. The "surplus-fragment" row (line 108) is "bindings belonging to no whole set" *for a given roster identity*, i.e., bindings on alice that don't fit any of alice's declared sets. Mason's bindings are on Mason, who is not iterated.

The finding asks for "a permanent carve-out." But the design **already has** the carve-out: line 134 is the carve-out. "Non-roster principals are out of scope" is exactly an explicit statement that the surplus scan does not touch Mason. The finding's suggested_fix option (b) — "define an explicit, named allow-set of system principals the surplus scan ignores" — is essentially restating what line 134 already does at the identity-set level (the roster *is* the allow-set of audited principals; everything else is ignored).

Is there a residual real concern? The finding gestures at a genuine second-order hazard in its fix: if the audit is identity-keyed and only visits roster members, then a binding **placed on a roster identity** (e.g., alice) by an attacker is caught, but a binding placed on a **fresh attacker-created SA** that is not in the roster is invisible. However, the memo addresses precisely this via the orphan-sweep / identity marker section (lines 136-147) — untracked SAs are a separate, acknowledged hazard with its own mechanism (SA tags / description sentinel). The finding does not engage with that section at all.

The claim that lines 29-31 and 134 are "unreconciled" is also weak. Lines 29-31 say system identities "reuse the capability-set abstraction" — meaning their grant bundles are *defined* using the same capability-set code construct — but explicitly "without the citizen lifecycle." Being a code construct for *defining* Mason's bindings does not entail being *in the declared roster* (which is data, line 85: "Capability-set definitions are code; the roster is data"). Mason's bindings being structured as a capability-set in code, while Mason has no roster entry and is out of audit scope, is fully consistent. The memo even flags the open question explicitly: "whether they deserve their own ontological category is deferred" (line 31).

So: the "unreconciled contradiction" the finding rests on does not exist; line 134 is itself the explicit reconciliation; and the suggested fix largely restates existing design. The finding misreads the audit as a global project-policy scan when the memo defines it as per-roster-identity.

Let me double check there's no place in the memo where the audit is described as a global scan that would catch Mason.

The relevant lines are 88 ("for each (identity, declared-set)"), 98 ("for one identity"), 106-108 (surplus rows defined per identity), and 134 (domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings). All four consistently describe a per-roster-identity audit. There is no global-scan description anywhere.

I have enough to render a verdict.

I read the cited evidence directly. All four citations are accurate as text: memo:134 ("Audit domain = roster-identities × set-defined bindings; non-roster principals are out of scope"), memo:29-31 (Mason et al. "sit outside it, reusing the capability-set abstraction without the citizen lifecycle"), RBSDE:192-197 (Mason's writer/objectViewer/viewer/logWriter grants), and RBSDK:161-165 (complete-policy-write re-asserts Mason's artifactregistry.writer). The factual substrate is sound.

But the finding's load-bearing claim — that the two memo statements are "unreconciled" and that "an audit that compares declared-roster identities against actual RBK-relevant bindings will read every Mason binding as surplus-fragment unless a permanent carve-out exists" — is a misreading of the audit's design. The memo defines the audit as PER-ROSTER-IDENTITY, not as a global project-policy scan. The drift taxonomy header (memo:98) is explicit: "for one identity; D = union of declared sets' bindings, A = actual RBK-relevant bindings." The surplus-fragment row (memo:108) is "bindings belonging to no whole set" FOR THAT ITERATED IDENTITY. The audit comparison (memo:88) is "for each (identity, declared-set)." And memo:134 caps the domain at "roster-identities × set-defined bindings." Mason is not in the declared roster (memo:25 — roster maps identity→capability-sets held; memo:29-31 places Mason outside the civic ontology and the citizen lifecycle), so the audit never visits Mason; its bindings are never examined and therefore cannot be classified as surplus-fragment. The surplus scan does not enumerate the whole project policy, which is the premise the finding requires.

The "unreconciled" charge fails on its own terms too: memo:134 IS the reconciliation — "non-roster principals are out of scope" is exactly an explicit, permanent statement that Mason's bindings are excluded. The finding asks for a "carve-out" that already exists. And memo:85 distinguishes capability-set *definitions* (code) from the *roster* (data); Mason's bindings being expressed via the capability-set code construct (memo:29-31, "reusing the capability-set abstraction") does NOT entail a roster entry, so there is no contradiction with being out of audit scope. The memo even flags the residual ontology question as a known open (memo:31, "whether they deserve their own ontological category is deferred") — which the prompt says not to re-report as "decide the scope."

The one genuine kernel — that an attacker could plant a binding on a fresh non-roster SA invisible to a roster-keyed audit — is real but is precisely the hazard the memo already addresses in its own dedicated section (memo:136-147, "Orphan hazard and the identity marker," via SA tags / description sentinel). The finding does not engage that section and so does not establish a gap. Verdict: false — the cited contradiction does not exist; line 134 is the explicit, already-present reconciliation, and the suggested fix (b) restates the existing roster-as-allow-set design.
input.refined_fix: No fix needed. Capability-set is a code construct for defining bindings (memo:85); Mason using it (memo:29-31) does not imply a roster entry, so there is no contradiction to reconcile. The only real adjacent hazard (attacker-planted binding on a fresh non-roster SA) is already owned by the orphan-marker section (memo:136-147). If the authors wanted to be belt-and-suspenders, a single clause at memo:134 noting "system identities (Mason/Envoy) are excluded here and covered by the orphan-marker sweep" would cross-link the two sections — but that is editorial polish, not a substantiated gap.
